title: Tyre Warm-Up And Cliff Change-Point Features
summary: F1 analysis shows pit timing depends on two nonlinear effects the baseline only approximates statically: fresh-tyre warm-up weakness can favor the overcut, while sudden old-tyre falloff can trigger an undercut or emergency stop.
rationale: F1 analysis shows pit timing depends on two nonlinear effects the baseline only approximates statically: fresh-tyre warm-up weakness can favor the overcut, while sudden old-tyre falloff can trigger an undercut or emergency stop. A causal onset detector should be more expressive than fixed expected-life constants.
implementation_hint: Within each Driver x Race x Stint sequence, compute prior-only rolling slope, acceleration, EWMA residual, and CUSUM-style change indicators for LapTime_Delta, Cumulative_Degradation, and race-relative pace residuals. Add track-compound warm-up priors learned from historical out-lap success by Race x Compound and interact them with current stint age and position.
expected_effect: Should catch the exact lap where degradation behavior changes, improving stop-timing precision beyond average tyre-life heuristics.
risk: Short stints and post-stop reset laps can create noisy false alarms. Mask the first few laps of each stint, require minimum history for change detectors, and verify gains with race-block CV rather than random folds.
